---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 18: Estimating average treatment effects (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 18](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-18-estimating-average-treatment-effects/).

Source text: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel
Data*, MIT Press — Chapter 18, "Estimating Average Treatment Effects". The data
files are those shipped with the book's own Stata distribution and are bundled
with `StatEcon`.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

The chapter's estimators map onto `StatEcon` as follows:

* `stata_regress`         — `regress y x…`
* `stata_probit`          — `probit d z…` (+ `predict p, p` via `GLM.predict`)
* `stata_summarize`       — `summarize var`
* `stata_ivregress_2sls`  — `ivreg y (x1 = z…) x2… [, robust]`

`StatEcon` also ships `psmatch2` (propensity-score matching) and
`teffects_ipw` (inverse-probability weighting). The UCLA page for this chapter
does **not** use them — it works entirely through regression on the propensity
score and IV — so they are not exercised here; the notebook stays faithful to
the published code.

```{julia}
jtrain2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("jtrain2"))   # 445 x 19
fertil2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("fertil2"))   # 4361 x 28
size(jtrain2), size(fertil2)
```

# Example 18.2 (page 619, jtrain2.dta)

## Propensity score for job-training assignment

```{julia}
#=
use jtrain2, clear

probit train re74 re75 age agesq nodegree married black hisp, nolog

Probit estimates                                  Number of obs   =        445
                                                  LR chi2(8)      =      16.07
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0415
Log likelihood = -294.06748                       Pseudo R2       =     0.0266

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       train |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        re74 |  -.0189577   .0159392    -1.19   0.234    -.0501979    .0122825
        re75 |   .0371871   .0271086     1.37   0.170    -.0159447     .090319
         age |  -.0005467   .0534045    -0.01   0.992    -.1052176    .1041242
       agesq |   .0000719   .0008734     0.08   0.934    -.0016399    .0017837
    nodegree |    -.44195   .1515457    -2.92   0.004    -.7389742   -.1449258
     married |    .091519   .1726192     0.53   0.596    -.2468083    .4298464
       black |  -.1446253   .2271609    -0.64   0.524    -.5898524    .3006019
        hisp |  -.5004545   .3079227    -1.63   0.104    -1.103972    .1030629
       _cons |   .2284561   .8154273     0.28   0.779    -1.369752    1.826664
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
pscore_18_2 = stata_probit(
    jtrain2,
    @formula(train ~ re74 + re75 + age + agesq + nodegree + married +
                     black + hisp));
```

All nine coefficients match the published table to six digits, as do
$N = 445$, LR $\chi^2(8) = 16.07$, log-likelihood $-294.0675$ and
pseudo-$R^2 = 0.0266$. Standard errors differ in the third significant digit
because `StatEcon`'s probit variance is the expected-information (IRLS) matrix
from `GLM.jl`, whereas Stata's `probit` reports the observed-information
inverse. Because the NSW data are from a randomised experiment, the covariates
should be — and essentially are — unrelated to assignment.

## Regression on the propensity score

```{julia}
#=
predict propensity, p
reg re78 train propensity

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     445
-------------+------------------------------           F(  2,   442) =    5.00
       Model |  432.165422     2  216.082711           Prob > F      =  0.0071
    Residual |  19093.4912   442  43.1979439           R-squared     =  0.0221
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0177
       Total |  19525.6566   444  43.9767041           Root MSE      =  6.5725

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        re78 |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       train |   1.625741   .6436153     2.53   0.012      .360815    2.890668
  propensity |    4.74316   3.398343     1.40   0.163    -1.935758    11.42208
       _cons |    2.65396   1.421591     1.87   0.063    -.1399571    5.447877
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
jtrain2.propensity = GLM.predict(pscore_18_2.model)   # predict propensity, p
reg_pscore = stata_regress(jtrain2; y = :re78, x = [:train, :propensity]);
```

`train` $= 1.6257445$ vs Stata's $1.625741$, SE $0.6436153$ in both;
`propensity` $4.7430552$ (Stata $4.74316$) with SE $3.3983295$ (Stata
$3.398343$); `_cons` $2.654003$ (Stata $2.65396$). $R^2 = 0.0221$ and Root MSE
$= 6.5725$ agree. The tiny fifth-digit drift on the propensity-score
coefficients is inherited from the probit fit (Newton–Raphson vs IRLS
convergence tolerances), not from the regression step.

## Regression on the covariates directly

```{julia}
#=
reg re78 train re74 re75 age agesq nodegree married black hisp

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     445
-------------+------------------------------           F(  9,   435) =    2.49
       Model |  955.909613     9  106.212179           Prob > F      =  0.0088
    Residual |   18569.747   435  42.6890736           R-squared     =  0.0490
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0293
       Total |  19525.6566   444  43.9767041           Root MSE      =  6.5337

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        re78 |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       train |   1.625166   .6399454     2.54   0.011     .3673965    2.882935
        re74 |   .0730643   .0775264     0.94   0.346    -.0793087    .2254373
        re75 |   .0651047   .1357642     0.48   0.632    -.2017307    .3319401
         age |   .2006381    .272712     0.74   0.462    -.3353589    .7366351
       agesq |  -.0025326   .0044565    -0.57   0.570    -.0112916    .0062264
    nodegree |  -1.099426   .7914955    -1.39   0.166    -2.655057    .4562048
     married |  -.1052808   .8930025    -0.12   0.906    -1.860417    1.649855
       black |  -2.115677   1.172482    -1.80   0.072     -4.42011     .188757
        hisp |  -.0095228   1.551503    -0.01   0.995    -3.058898    3.039852
       _cons |   3.691303   4.180235     0.88   0.378    -4.524666    11.90727
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
reg_covs = stata_regress(
    jtrain2;
    y = :re78,
    x = [:train, :re74, :re75, :age, :agesq, :nodegree, :married,
         :black, :hisp]);
```

An exact reproduction: `train` $=1.625166$ (SE $0.6399454$), $R^2 = 0.0490$,
Root MSE $= 6.5337$, $F(9,435) = 2.49$. Controlling for the covariates directly
and controlling for the single-index propensity score give virtually the same
ATE estimate — the hallmark of a randomised experiment.

## Simple difference in means

```{julia}
#=
reg re78 train /*simple comparison*/

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     445
-------------+------------------------------           F(  1,   443) =    8.04
       Model |  348.013451     1  348.013451           Prob > F      =  0.0048
    Residual |  19177.6432   443  43.2903909           R-squared     =  0.0178
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0156
       Total |  19525.6566   444  43.9767041           Root MSE      =  6.5795

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        re78 |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       train |   1.794343   .6328536     2.84   0.005     .5505749    3.038111
       _cons |   4.554802    .408046    11.16   0.000     3.752856    5.356749
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
reg_simple = stata_regress(jtrain2; y = :re78, x = [:train]);
```

Exact match: the raw difference in means is $1.794343$ (SE $0.6328536$),
somewhat larger than the covariate-adjusted $1.625$.

## Propensity score summary and the interacted regression

```{julia}
#=
sum propensity

    Variable |       Obs        Mean    Std. Dev.       Min        Max
-------------+--------------------------------------------------------
  propensity |       445    .4155321    .0934459   .1638736   .6738951

gen wp = train*(propensity-r(mean))
reg re78 train propensity wp /*adding the interaction term*/

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     445
-------------+------------------------------           F(  3,   441) =    4.61
       Model |  593.815328     3  197.938443           Prob > F      =  0.0034
    Residual |  18931.8413   441  42.9293453           R-squared     =  0.0304
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0238
       Total |  19525.6566   444  43.9767041           Root MSE      =   6.552

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        re78 |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       train |   1.554095   .6426726     2.42   0.016     .2910138    2.817177
  propensity |  -.9943707   4.496586    -0.22   0.825    -9.831772     7.84303
          wp |   13.26973   6.838352     1.94   0.053    -.1700793    26.70954
       _cons |   4.953301   1.847272     2.68   0.008      1.32275    8.583851
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
stata_summarize(jtrain2, [:propensity])

jtrain2.wp = jtrain2.train .* (jtrain2.propensity .-
                               Statistics.mean(jtrain2.propensity))
reg_inter = stata_regress(jtrain2; y = :re78, x = [:train, :propensity, :wp]);
```

Summary statistics match: mean $0.4155319$ (Stata $0.4155321$), SD $0.0934463$
(Stata $0.0934459$), min $0.1638668$ (Stata $0.1638736$), max $0.6739013$
(Stata $0.6738951$) — the sixth-digit drift again comes from the probit
optimiser. In the interacted regression the ATE (`train`) is $1.5540979$ vs
Stata's $1.554095$, `propensity` $-0.9944404$ (Stata $-0.9943707$), `wp`
$13.2696839$ (Stata $13.26973$), `_cons` $4.9533285$ (Stata $4.953301$), with
$R^2 = 0.0304$ and Root MSE $= 6.552$. Because `wp` is centred at the sample
mean of the propensity score, the `train` coefficient is the estimated ATE.

# Example 18.3 (page 624, fertil2.dta)

Here the "treatment" is `educ7` (at least seven years of schooling) and the
outcome is `children`. `frsthalf` — born in the first half of the year — is the
instrument.

```{julia}
# `electric` and `tv` carry a handful of missing values; the estimation sample
# is the 4,358 complete cases Stata uses.
fert = DataFrames.dropmissing(
    fertil2[:, [:children, :educ7, :educ, :age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban,
                :electric, :tv, :frsthalf]])
DataFrames.nrow(fert)
```

## OLS

```{julia}
#=
use fertil2, clear

reg children educ7 age agesq evermarr urban electric tv

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =    4358
-------------+------------------------------           F(  7,  4350) =  880.03
       Model |  12607.4006     7  1801.05723           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  8902.63153  4350  2.04658196           R-squared     =  0.5861
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.5855
       Total |  21510.0321  4357  4.93689055           Root MSE      =  1.4306

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    children |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       educ7 |  -.3935524   .0495534    -7.94   0.000    -.4907024   -.2964025
         age |   .2719307   .0171033    15.90   0.000     .2383996    .3054618
       agesq |   -.001896   .0002752    -6.89   0.000    -.0024356   -.0013564
    evermarr |   .6947417   .0523984    13.26   0.000     .5920142    .7974691
       urban |  -.2437082   .0460252    -5.30   0.000     -.333941   -.1534753
    electric |   -.336644   .0754557    -4.46   0.000    -.4845756   -.1887124
          tv |  -.3259749   .0897716    -3.63   0.000     -.501973   -.1499767
       _cons |  -3.526605   .2451026   -14.39   0.000    -4.007131   -3.046079
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
ols_18_3 = stata_regress(
    fert;
    y = :children,
    x = [:educ7, :age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban, :electric, :tv]);
```

Digit-for-digit reproduction of the published table, including
$N = 4358$, $F(7,4350) = 880.03$, $R^2 = 0.5861$ and Root MSE $= 1.4306$.

## IV using the raw instrument

```{julia}
#=
ivreg children (educ7 =frsthalf ) age agesq evermarr urban electric tv

Instrumental variables (2SLS) regression

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =    4358
-------------+------------------------------           F(  7,  4350) =  829.33
       Model |  12154.5373     7  1736.36247           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  9355.49483  4350  2.15068847           R-squared     =  0.5651
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.5644
       Total |  21510.0321  4357  4.93689055           Root MSE      =  1.4665

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    children |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       educ7 |   -1.13068   .6192352    -1.83   0.068    -2.344696    .0833367
         age |   .2627018     .01916    13.71   0.000     .2251385    .3002651
       agesq |  -.0019787   .0002905    -6.81   0.000    -.0025483   -.0014091
    evermarr |   .6167576   .0845468     7.29   0.000     .4510028    .7825123
       urban |  -.1672413   .0795281    -2.10   0.036    -.3231569   -.0113257
    electric |  -.2343255   .1154192    -2.03   0.042     -.460606   -.0080451
          tv |  -.1371643   .1829146    -0.75   0.453    -.4957701    .2214415
       _cons |   -2.83005   .6350035    -4.46   0.000     -4.07498    -1.58512
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Instrumented:  educ7
Instruments:   age agesq evermarr urban electric tv frsthalf
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
iv_raw = stata_ivregress_2sls(
    fert, :children, :educ7 => :frsthalf,
    [:age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban, :electric, :tv];
    robust = false, first = false);
```

Exact match on every coefficient and standard error (`educ7` $-1.1306797$
against Stata's $-1.13068$, SE $0.6192352$), plus $R^2 = 0.5651$ and Root MSE
$= 1.4665$. Instrumenting the treatment dummy nearly triples the estimated
effect of schooling on fertility, but it is only marginally significant. The
only display difference is that `stata_ivregress_2sls` reports large-sample
$z$/Wald statistics where the old `ivreg` reported $t$/$F$.

## Procedure 18.1: the fitted probability as the instrument

```{julia}
#=
probit educ7 frsthalf age agesq evermarr urban electric tv, nolog

Probit estimates                                  Number of obs   =       4358
                                                  LR chi2(7)      =    1130.84
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0000
Log likelihood =  -2428.384                       Pseudo R2       =     0.1889

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       educ7 |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    frsthalf |  -.2206627   .0418563    -5.27   0.000    -.3026995   -.1386259
         age |  -.0150337   .0174845    -0.86   0.390    -.0493027    .0192354
       agesq |  -.0007325   .0002897    -2.53   0.011    -.0013003   -.0001647
    evermarr |  -.2972879   .0486734    -6.11   0.000     -.392686   -.2018898
       urban |   .2998122   .0432321     6.93   0.000     .2150789    .3845456
    electric |   .4246668   .0751255     5.65   0.000     .2774235      .57191
          tv |   .9281707   .0977462     9.50   0.000     .7365915     1.11975
       _cons |    1.13537   .2440057     4.65   0.000     .6571273    1.613612
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

predict propensity, p /*procedure 18.1*/
(3 missing values generated)
=#
```

```{julia}
pscore_18_3 = stata_probit(
    fert,
    @formula(educ7 ~ frsthalf + age + agesq + evermarr + urban + electric + tv))

fert.propensity = GLM.predict(pscore_18_3.model);
```

Coefficients match to five–six digits (`frsthalf` $-0.220663$ vs $-0.2206627$,
`tv` $0.9281441$ vs $0.9281707$), and $N = 4358$, LR $\chi^2(7) = 1130.84$,
log-likelihood $-2428.384$, pseudo-$R^2 = 0.1889$ are identical. Once again the
standard errors are the expected-information versions and differ from Stata's
observed-information ones by 0.1–2%.

## IV using the propensity score

```{julia}
#=
ivreg children (educ7= propensity)  age agesq evermarr urban electric tv

Instrumental variables (2SLS) regression

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =    4358
-------------+------------------------------           F(  7,  4350) =  710.92
       Model |  10524.2446     7  1503.46351           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  10985.7876  4350  2.52546841           R-squared     =  0.4893
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.4884
       Total |  21510.0321  4357  4.93689055           Root MSE      =  1.5892

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    children |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       educ7 |  -1.974509    .331779    -5.95   0.000    -2.624964   -1.324053
         age |    .252137   .0194358    12.97   0.000     .2140329     .290241
       agesq |  -.0020734   .0003079    -6.73   0.000    -.0026772   -.0014697
    evermarr |    .527485   .0677212     7.79   0.000     .3947169    .6602531
       urban |  -.0797056   .0613673    -1.30   0.194    -.2000168    .0406056
    electric |  -.1171961   .0953328    -1.23   0.219    -.3040969    .0697047
          tv |   .0789773   .1302613     0.61   0.544    -.1764013    .3343558
       _cons |  -2.032667   .4119708    -4.93   0.000    -2.840339   -1.224994
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Instrumented:  educ7
Instruments:   age agesq evermarr urban electric tv propensity
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
iv_ps = stata_ivregress_2sls(
    fert, :children, :educ7 => :propensity,
    [:age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban, :electric, :tv];
    robust = false, first = false);
```

`educ7` $=-1.9745012$ against Stata's $-1.974509$, SE $0.3317810$ vs
$0.331779$; every other coefficient and standard error matches to six digits,
as do $R^2 = 0.4893$ and Root MSE $= 1.5892$. Using the fitted probability as
the single instrument (Procedure 18.1) roughly halves the standard error
relative to using `frsthalf` directly.

## The same IV with robust standard errors

```{julia}
#=
ivreg children (educ7= propensity)  age agesq evermarr urban electric tv, robust

IV (2SLS) regression with robust standard errors       Number of obs =    4358
                                                       F(  7,  4350) =  678.11
                                                       Prob > F      =  0.0000
                                                       R-squared     =  0.4893
                                                       Root MSE      =  1.5892

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
    children |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       educ7 |  -1.974509   .3135566    -6.30   0.000    -2.589239   -1.359778
         age |    .252137   .0210049    12.00   0.000     .2109566    .2933173
       agesq |  -.0020734   .0003816    -5.43   0.000    -.0028215   -.0013254
    evermarr |    .527485   .0695789     7.58   0.000      .391075     .663895
       urban |  -.0797056   .0605259    -1.32   0.188    -.1983672     .038956
    electric |  -.1171961   .0891859    -1.31   0.189    -.2920458    .0576536
          tv |   .0789773   .1084846     0.73   0.467    -.1337078    .2916623
       _cons |  -2.032667   .3642986    -5.58   0.000    -2.746877   -1.318456
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Instrumented:  educ7
Instruments:   age agesq evermarr urban electric tv propensity
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
iv_ps_rob = stata_ivregress_2sls(
    fert, :children, :educ7 => :propensity,
    [:age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban, :electric, :tv];
    robust = true, first = false);
```

The robust standard errors reproduce Stata to six digits as well
(`educ7` $0.3135591$ vs $0.3135566$; `age` $0.0210049$ in both; `_cons`
$0.3642998$ vs $0.3642986$). Note that these standard errors — like Stata's —
do *not* correct for the fact that `propensity` is a generated instrument;
Wooldridge points out that no correction is needed here because the propensity
score enters only as an instrument.

# Comparison table: Stata vs Julia

```{julia}
b(m)      = StatsBase.coef(m)
nms(m)    = string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m))
pick(m,s) = b(m)[findfirst(==(s), nms(m))]
se(m,s)   = sqrt(LinearAlgebra.diag(StatsBase.vcov(m))[findfirst(==(s), nms(m))])

comparison = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    example = ["18.2 probit","18.2 probit","18.2 probit",
               "18.2 reg on p","18.2 reg on p","18.2 reg on p",
               "18.2 reg on x","18.2 reg on x",
               "18.2 simple","18.2 simple",
               "18.2 sum p","18.2 sum p",
               "18.2 interacted","18.2 interacted","18.2 interacted",
               "18.3 OLS","18.3 OLS","18.3 OLS",
               "18.3 IV frsthalf","18.3 IV frsthalf","18.3 IV frsthalf",
               "18.3 probit","18.3 probit",
               "18.3 IV pscore","18.3 IV pscore","18.3 IV pscore",
               "18.3 IV pscore robust","18.3 IV pscore robust"],
    quantity = ["nodegree","hisp","log-lik",
                "train","train SE","propensity",
                "train","train SE",
                "train","_cons",
                "mean p","sd p",
                "train","wp","_cons",
                "educ7","evermarr","R-squared",
                "educ7","educ7 SE","_cons",
                "frsthalf","log-lik",
                "educ7","educ7 SE","tv",
                "educ7 SE","age SE"],
    stata = [-0.44195, -0.5004545, -294.06748,
             1.625741, 0.6436153, 4.74316,
             1.625166, 0.6399454,
             1.794343, 4.554802,
             0.4155321, 0.0934459,
             1.554095, 13.26973, 4.953301,
             -0.3935524, 0.6947417, 0.5861,
             -1.13068, 0.6192352, -2.83005,
             -0.2206627, -2428.384,
             -1.974509, 0.331779, 0.0789773,
             0.3135566, 0.0210049],
    julia = [pscore_18_2.β[5], pscore_18_2.β[8], pscore_18_2.ll,
             pick(reg_pscore,"train"), se(reg_pscore,"train"),
             pick(reg_pscore,"propensity"),
             pick(reg_covs,"train"), se(reg_covs,"train"),
             pick(reg_simple,"train"), pick(reg_simple,"(Intercept)"),
             Statistics.mean(jtrain2.propensity),
             Statistics.std(jtrain2.propensity),
             pick(reg_inter,"train"), pick(reg_inter,"wp"),
             pick(reg_inter,"(Intercept)"),
             pick(ols_18_3,"educ7"), pick(ols_18_3,"evermarr"),
             StatsBase.r2(ols_18_3),
             pick(iv_raw.iv,"educ7"), se(iv_raw.iv,"educ7"),
             pick(iv_raw.iv,"(Intercept)"),
             pscore_18_3.β[1], pscore_18_3.ll,
             pick(iv_ps.iv,"educ7"), se(iv_ps.iv,"educ7"), pick(iv_ps.iv,"tv"),
             se(iv_ps_rob.iv,"educ7"), se(iv_ps_rob.iv,"age")])
comparison.match = [abs(s - j) <= 1e-3 * max(1.0e-3, abs(s)) for (s, j) in
                    zip(comparison.stata, comparison.julia)]
comparison
```

Every point estimate agrees with the published Stata output to at least four
significant digits, and every OLS/2SLS standard error (classical and robust)
agrees to at least five. The single systematic difference in this chapter is in
the **probit standard errors**, where `StatEcon`/`GLM.jl` report the
expected-information variance and Stata reports the observed-information one.
